descriptions: to definitely decide, arrange, or complete something
to fix something firmly in position with a nail or nails
to make someone agree to something or tell you what they are going to do
examples: Friends in high places who could nail down a summer rental for me.
I’ll try to nail Jim down about the date.
They’re on the verge of getting the agreement nailed down.
highlighted_examples: Friends in high places who could [nail] [down] a summer rental for me.
I’ll try to [nail] Jim [down] about the date.
They’re on the verge of getting the agreement [nailed] [down].

descriptions: to fix something to a wall with a nail or nails
to use nails to fasten a door or window firmly shut
examples: A few minutes of solace... Peter closed and locked the door behind him, then replaced the house keys on a nail up under the porch rafters.
highlighted_examples: A few minutes of solace... Peter closed and locked the door behind him, then replaced the house keys on a [nail] [up] under the porch rafters.

descriptions: to give someone or something the same name as someone or something else, especially the same first name as a member of your family
examples: Albert was named after his grandfather.
highlighted_examples: Albert was [named] [after] his grandfather.

descriptions: to give a place or an object the same name as a person or another thing
examples: The prize is named for the late Mexican writer, Juan Rulfo.
highlighted_examples: The prize is [named] [for] the late Mexican writer, Juan Rulfo.

descriptions: to reduce the number of possibilities or choices
examples: That narrowed it down to just two possibilities.
We’re working to narrow down the list of possible suspects.
highlighted_examples: That [narrowed] it [down] to just two possibilities.
We’re working to [narrow] [down] the list of possible suspects.

descriptions: to reduce the total amount of something by gradually using small amounts of it
examples: Beware of hidden costs that can nibble away at your budget.
highlighted_examples: Beware of hidden costs that can [nibble] [away] [at] your budget.

descriptions: to go to sleep, especially when you do not intend to
examples: As the scotch took hold of my brain and I started to nod off I fell into a dream.
At her age she ought to have been allowed to nod off in some warm chimney nook.
He even kept a camp bed there for the nights when he was so exhausted he would begin to nod off as he worked.
I drank it - I was feeling sleepy now, and it wouldn't do to nod off in Florence's drawing room.
highlighted_examples: As the scotch took hold of my brain and I started to [nod] [off] I fell into a dream.
At her age she ought to have been allowed to [nod] [off] in some warm chimney nook.
He even kept a camp bed there for the nights when he was so exhausted he would begin to [nod] [off] as he worked.
I drank it - I was feeling sleepy now, and it wouldn't do to [nod] [off] in Florence's drawing room.

descriptions: to try to find out information about someone or something
examples: A few kids were nosing around.
I found two men nosing around the boat.
highlighted_examples: A few kids were [nosing] around.
I found two men [nosing] around the boat.

descriptions: to try to find out information about someone or something
examples: A few kids were nosing around.
I found two men nosing around the boat.
highlighted_examples: A few kids were [nosing] [around].
I found two men [nosing] [around] the boat.

descriptions: to try to find out information about something
examples: I don’t want them nosing into my finances.
highlighted_examples: I don’t want them [nosing] [into] my finances.

descriptions: to find out information, especially something that someone does not want you to know
examples: We’ve managed to nose out a few things that I’m sure he wouldn’t want to be made public.
highlighted_examples: We’ve managed to [nose] [out] a few things that I’m sure he wouldn’t want to be made public.

descriptions: to win something, or to achieve something
examples: The company notched up over £10 million in profits last year.
highlighted_examples: The company [notched] [up] over £10 million in profits last year.

descriptions: to be part of a particular group, or to include someone or something among a particular group
examples: He numbers among America’s top authors.
I number him among my friends.
highlighted_examples: He [numbers] [among] America’s top authors.
I [number] him [among] my friends.

descriptions: to give each person in a group a number that is used for referring to them
examples: The children stand in a line and are numbered off.
highlighted_examples: The children stand in a line and are [numbered] [off].
